1→# Phase 2: Operating — Build the Prospect Intelligence Framework
     2→
     3→**Who:** 500+ outbound, CRM connected, solo or small team.
     4→**Data source:** Data-first. Rich email history + CRM pipeline. Show them what they don't know.
     5→**Philosophy:** Reveal hidden patterns, then add intelligence on top.
     6→
     7→## Resume Keys
     8→
     9→Same as phase-2-zero.md (S1-S10 steps use identical config keys).
    10→
    11→## Pre-Step: Deep Email Analysis
    12→
    13→With 500+ emails, present a full performance analysis before the intelligence build:
    14→
    15→"[Name], I analyzed [X] outbound emails. Here's what the data says:
    16→
    17→WHO REPLIES (not just who you email):
    18→| Persona         | Emails | Reply Rate |
    19→|-----------------|--------|------------|
    20→| [Title 1]       | [N]    | [X]%      |
    21→| [Title 2]       | [N]    | [X]%      |
    22→| [Title 3]       | [N]    | [X]%      |
    23→
    24→WHAT WORKS:
    25→- Subject lines that get opens: [pattern]
    26→- Openers that get replies: [pattern]
    27→- Best CTA style: [pattern]
    28→- Best day/time: [pattern]
    29→
    30→SURPRISES:
    31→- [Persona] replies at [X]% -- higher than [Persona] despite fewer emails
    32→- Emails mentioning [topic] get [X]x more replies
    33→- Your reply rate drops after [X] follow-ups
    34→
    35→Let me build your intelligence framework using these patterns."
    36→
    37→---
    38→
    39→## How Operating Differs From Zero
    40→
    41→The S1-S10 steps follow the **same structure** as phase-2-zero.md. Read that file for full step definitions. Below are ONLY the differences:
    42→
    43→### S1: Disqualifiers
    44→**DATA-DRIVEN:** "Companies in [industry/size] never reply to you (0% over [N] emails). Skip them?"
    45→
    46→### S2: ICP
    47→**FROM REPLY DATA, not just send data:**
    48→"You email VP Sales the most (340 emails) but RevOps replies at 15% vs VP Sales at 12%. Your real ICP might be different from who you think it is. Want to lean into what works?"
    49→
    50→### S3: Personas + Buying Committee
    51→**PART A personas:** Ranked by reply rate AND deal conversion (CRM data).
    52→"Your top 3 personas by actual conversion:
    53→1. RevOps -- 15% reply, 2.5% deal rate
    54→2. VP Sales -- 12% reply, 0.7% deal rate
    55→3. Founder -- 8% reply, 0.8% deal rate
    56→
    57→Surprise: RevOps converts 3.5x better than VP Sales."
    58→
    59→**PART B buying committee:** Rich CC/thread data from 500+ emails.
    60→"Your email threads show a clear buying committee pattern:
    61→- You email [Champion] first (100% of threads)
    62→- [Evaluator] gets CC'd after [X] exchanges ([Y]% of threads)
    63→- [Blocker] joins in [Z]% of threads (usually asks about [topic])
    64→- [Approver] appears in [W]% of threads (usually near close)
    65→
    66→Average thread length before deal: [N] emails across [M] people."
    67→
    68→### S4: Transformation Models
    69→**DATA-PROVEN patterns:**
    70→"For RevOps (your best-converting persona):
    71→- Your emails that convert mention 'data hygiene' and 'pipeline reporting'
    72→- You never pitch the product in first email -- you ask about their stack
    73→- Replies come when you name a specific tool they use
    74→
    75→Transformation model: 'RevOps spends 10+ hours/week cleaning CRM data.
    76→Your product automates the cleanup at the source.'
    77→
    78→This matches what works in your actual emails. Hit close?"
    79→
    80→### S5: Timing Signals
    81→**CALIBRATED by actual reply data:**
    82→"Here's what predicts replies in YOUR data:
    83→- Recently funded companies: 18% reply rate (vs 8% average)
    84→- Companies with >3 SDRs: 14% reply rate
    85→- Companies using [competitor]: 16% reply rate
    86→
    87→I'll prioritize these signals based on your proven data, not industry averages."
    88→
    89→### S6: Insight Priorities
    90→Same as zero, but validated: "Your emails with people insights (naming team members) get [X]x more replies than generic ones. Confirms people insight as #1 priority."
    91→
    92→### S7: Voice + Positioning
    93→**Fully extracted with performance correlation:**
    94→"Your voice profile from [X] emails:
    95→- Formality: [X]/10
    96→- Avg email: [X] words
    97→- Never uses: [patterns]
    98→- Always uses: [patterns]
    99→- Best opener: [specific pattern with reply rate]
   100→- Best CTA: '[exact phrase]' ([X]% reply rate vs [Y]% for alternatives)
   101→
   102→Your best-performing email: [show it with analysis of why it worked]"
   103→
   104→### S8-S10: Same as zero.
   105→

1→# Phase 2: Scaling — Build the Prospect Intelligence Framework
     2→
     3→**Who:** 500+ outbound, CRM connected, team >1 person.
     4→**Data source:** Cross-team email data + CRM pipeline. Team-wide intelligence.
     5→**Philosophy:** Find what each rep does best, make the whole team that good.
     6→
     7→## Resume Keys
     8→
     9→Same as phase-2-zero.md (S1-S10 steps use identical config keys).
    10→
    11→## Pre-Step: Team + Cross-Rep Analysis
    12→
    13→"[Name], I analyzed [X] emails from your connected mailbox. Your CRM shows [Y] reps on the team.
    14→
    15→TEAM PERFORMANCE:
    16→| Metric          | You     | Team Avg | Best Rep |
    17→|-----------------|---------|----------|----------|
    18→| Reply rate      | [X]%    | [Y]%     | [Z]%    |
    19→| Emails/day      | [X]     | [Y]      | [Z]     |
    20→| Meetings/week   | [X]     | [Y]      | [Z]     |
    21→
    22→WHO YOUR TEAM TARGETS vs WHO CONVERTS:
    23→| Persona         | Emails | Deals  | Win Rate   |
    24→|-----------------|--------|--------|------------|
    25→| [Title 1]       | [N]    | [N]    | [X]%       |
    26→| [Title 2]       | [N]    | [N]    | [X]%       |
    27→| [Title 3]       | [N]    | [N]    | [X]%       |
    28→
    29→CROSS-REP PATTERNS:
    30→- [Rep A] is best at [Persona] emails ([X]% win rate)
    31→- [Rep B] is best at [Persona] emails ([X]% win rate)
    32→- You are best at [Persona] ([X]% win rate)
    33→
    34→Let me build a framework that captures what your BEST rep does for each persona."
    35→
    36→**Note:** `nurge sync` only supports one Gmail mailbox. Cross-rep analysis uses CRM data (deal owner, pipeline stages) plus the connected mailbox. Multi-mailbox sync is future work.
    37→
    38→---
    39→
    40→## How Scaling Differs From Zero
    41→
    42→The S1-S10 steps follow the **same structure** as phase-2-zero.md. Read that file for full step definitions. Below are ONLY the differences:
    43→
    44→### S1: Disqualifiers
    45→**TEAM-CALIBRATED:** "Across all reps, companies in [segment] never convert (0 deals from [N] emails). Team-wide skip?"
    46→
    47→### S2: ICP
    48→**FROM CRM DEAL DATA:**
    49→"Your CRM shows what actually closes, not just what gets emailed:
    50→- [Segment] closes at [X]% -- highest win rate
    51→- [Segment] gets the most emails but [X]% win rate -- low
    52→
    53→Should the team focus more on [high-converting segment]?"
    54→
    55→### S3: Personas + Buying Committee
    56→**PART A: Per-rep strengths.**
    57→"Different reps succeed with different personas:
    58→- [Rep A]: best at [VP Eng] (2.1% win rate)
    59→- [Rep B]: best at [Founder] (1.4% win rate)
    60→- You: best at [RevOps] (3.2% win rate)
    61→
    62→I'll encode each rep's best approach into the framework."
    63→
    64→**PART B: Team-wide committee pattern from CRM + emails.**
    65→"Across all deals in your CRM, the buying committee looks like:
    66→- Champion: [role] (contacted first in [X]% of won deals)
    67→- Evaluator: [role] (appears by stage [X])
    68→- Blocker: [role] (appears by stage [X])
    69→- Average deal involves [N] people over [M] emails"
    70→
    71→### S4: Transformation Models
    72→**BEST-REP APPROACH per persona:**
    73→"For VP Eng, [Rep A] closes at 2.1%. Here's what [Rep A] does differently:
    74→- Opens with [pattern] (vs team average of [pattern])
    75→- Mentions [topic] that other reps don't
    76→- Uses [CTA style]
    77→
    78→I'll make this the transformation model for VP Eng so the whole team writes at [Rep A]'s level."
    79→
    80→### S5: Timing Signals
    81→**TEAM-CALIBRATED:**
    82→"Across all reps:
    83→- Funded companies: [Rep A] closes 3x more with these
    84→- Hiring companies: you close 2x more
    85→- Competitor users: [Rep B] handles best
    86→
    87→I'll route signal-based insights to match each rep's strength."
    88→
    89→### S6: Insight Priorities
    90→Same structure, but per-rep preferences noted: "[Rep A] gets better results with tool insights, you get better with people insights."
    91→
    92→### S7: Voice + Positioning
    93→**PER-REP voice profiles:**
    94→"I found [N] different writing styles across your team:
    95→- You: [style summary]
    96→- [Rep A]: [style summary]
    97→- [Rep B]: [style summary]
    98→
    99→All work. I'll match each rep's voice when writing on their behalf.
   100→Or should we standardize on one style?"

1→package learning
     2→
     3→import (
     4→	"database/sql"
     5→	"encoding/json"
     6→	"fmt"
     7→	"os"
     8→	"path/filepath"
     9→)
    10→
    11→// Learning represents a single insight captured from a Claude Code session.
    12→type Learning struct {
    13→	ID            int    `json:"id"`
    14→	Type          string `json:"type"`
    15→	Content       string `json:"content"`
    16→	SourceSession string `json:"source_session,omitempty"`
    17→	CreatedAt     string `json:"created_at"`
    18→}
    19→
    20→// SkillVersion represents a versioned snapshot of a skill.
    21→type SkillVersion struct {
    22→	ID            int    `json:"id"`
    23→	SkillName     string `json:"skill_name"`
    24→	Version       int    `json:"version"`
    25→	Content       string `json:"content"`
    26→	EmailCount    int    `json:"email_count"`
    27→	LearningCount int    `json:"learning_count"`
    28→	Active        bool   `json:"active"`
    29→	CreatedAt     string `json:"created_at"`
    30→}
    31→
    32→// SkillSummary is a brief view of a skill with its current version.
    33→type SkillSummary struct {
    34→	SkillName      string `json:"skill_name"`
    35→	ActiveVersion  int    `json:"active_version"`
    36→	TotalVersions  int    `json:"total_versions"`
    37→	LastUpdated    string `json:"last_updated"`
    38→}
    39→
    40→// SaveLearning inserts a new learning into the database.
    41→func SaveLearning(db *sql.DB, typ, content, sourceSession string) (int64, error) {
    42→	result, err := db.Exec(
    43→		`INSERT INTO learnings (type, content, source_session) VALUES (?, ?, ?)`,
    44→		typ, content, sourceSession,
    45→	)
    46→	if err != nil {
    47→		return 0, fmt.Errorf("saving learning: %w", err)
    48→	}
    49→	return result.LastInsertId()
    50→}
    51→
    52→// ListLearnings returns all learnings, optionally filtered by type.
    53→func ListLearnings(db *sql.DB, filterType string) ([]Learning, error) {
    54→	var rows *sql.Rows
    55→	var err error
    56→
    57→	if filterType != "" {
    58→		rows, err = db.Query(
    59→			`SELECT id, type, content, source_session, created_at FROM learnings WHERE type = ? ORDER BY created_at DESC`,
    60→			filterType,
    61→		)
    62→	} else {
    63→		rows, err = db.Query(
    64→			`SELECT id, type, content, source_session, created_at FROM learnings ORDER BY created_at DESC`,
    65→		)
    66→	}
    67→	if err != nil {
    68→		return nil, fmt.Errorf("listing learnings: %w", err)
    69→	}
    70→	defer rows.Close()
    71→
    72→	var learnings []Learning
    73→	for rows.Next() {
    74→		var l Learning
    75→		if err := rows.Scan(&l.ID, &l.Type, &l.Content, &l.SourceSession, &l.CreatedAt); err != nil {
    76→			return nil, fmt.Errorf("scanning learning: %w", err)
    77→		}
    78→		learnings = append(learnings, l)
    79→	}
    80→	return learnings, rows.Err()
    81→}
    82→
    83→// SaveSkillVersion saves a new version of a skill. It deactivates all previous
    84→// versions and marks the new one as active.
    85→func SaveSkillVersion(db *sql.DB, skillName, content string, emailCount, learningCount int) (int, error) {
    86→	tx, err := db.Begin()
    87→	if err != nil {
    88→		return 0, fmt.Errorf("beginning transaction: %w", err)
    89→	}
    90→	defer tx.Rollback()
    91→
    92→	// Get next version number
    93→	var maxVersion sql.NullInt64
    94→	err = tx.QueryRow(
    95→		`SELECT MAX(version) FROM skill_versions WHERE skill_name = ?`,
    96→		skillName,
    97→	).Scan(&maxVersion)
    98→	if err != nil {
    99→		return 0, fmt.Errorf("getting max version: %w", err)
   100→	}
   101→
   102→	nextVersion := 1
   103→	if maxVersion.Valid {
   104→		nextVersion = int(maxVersion.Int64) + 1
   105→	}
   106→
   107→	// Deactivate all previous versions
   108→	_, err = tx.Exec(
   109→		`UPDATE skill_versions SET active = 0 WHERE skill_name = ?`,
   110→		skillName,
   111→	)
   112→	if err != nil {
   113→		return 0, fmt.Errorf("deactivating versions: %w", err)
   114→	}
   115→
   116→	// Insert new version as active
   117→	_, err = tx.Exec(
   118→		`INSERT INTO skill_versions (skill_name, version, content, email_count, learning_count, active) VALUES (?, ?, ?, ?, ?, 1)`,
   119→		skillName, nextVersion, content, emailCount, learningCount,
   120→	)
   121→	if err != nil {
   122→		return 0, fmt.Errorf("inserting version: %w", err)
   123→	}
   124→
   125→	if err := tx.Commit(); err != nil {
   126→		return 0, fmt.Errorf("committing transaction: %w", err)
   127→	}
   128→
   129→	return nextVersion, nil
   130→}
   131→
   132→// ListSkills returns a summary of all skills with their active version info.
   133→func ListSkills(db *sql.DB) ([]SkillSummary, error) {
   134→	rows, err := db.Query(`
   135→		SELECT
   136→			sv.skill_name,
   137→			COALESCE(active.version, 0) AS active_version,
   138→			COUNT(*) AS total_versions,
   139→			MAX(sv.created_at) AS last_updated
   140→		FROM skill_versions sv
   141→		LEFT JOIN skill_versions active
   142→			ON active.skill_name = sv.skill_name AND active.active = 1
   143→		GROUP BY sv.skill_name
   144→		ORDER BY sv.skill_name
   145→	`)
   146→	if err != nil {
   147→		return nil, fmt.Errorf("listing skills: %w", err)
   148→	}
   149→	defer rows.Close()
   150→
   151→	var skills []SkillSummary
   152→	for rows.Next() {
   153→		var s SkillSummary
   154→		if err := rows.Scan(&s.SkillName, &s.ActiveVersion, &s.TotalVersions, &s.LastUpdated); err != nil {
   155→			return nil, fmt.Errorf("scanning skill: %w", err)
   156→		}
   157→		skills = append(skills, s)
   158→	}
   159→	return skills, rows.Err()
   160→}
   161→
   162→// SkillHistory returns all versions of a skill, newest first.
   163→func SkillHistory(db *sql.DB, skillName string) ([]SkillVersion, error) {
   164→	rows, err := db.Query(
   165→		`SELECT id, skill_name, version, content, email_count, learning_count, active, created_at
   166→		 FROM skill_versions WHERE skill_name = ? ORDER BY version DESC`,
   167→		skillName,
   168→	)
   169→	if err != nil {
   170→		return nil, fmt.Errorf("getting skill history: %w", err)
   171→	}
   172→	defer rows.Close()
   173→
   174→	var versions []SkillVersion
   175→	for rows.Next() {
   176→		var v SkillVersion
   177→		if err := rows.Scan(&v.ID, &v.SkillName, &v.Version, &v.Content, &v.EmailCount, &v.LearningCount, &v.Active, &v.CreatedAt); err != nil {
   178→			return nil, fmt.Errorf("scanning version: %w", err)
   179→		}
   180→		versions = append(versions, v)
   181→	}
   182→	return versions, rows.Err()
   183→}
   184→
   185→// RollbackSkill restores a previous version of a skill by marking it as active
   186→// and writing its content to disk.
   187→func RollbackSkill(db *sql.DB, skillName string, version int) error {
   188→	tx, err := db.Begin()
   189→	if err != nil {
   190→		return fmt.Errorf("beginning transaction: %w", err)
   191→	}
   192→	defer tx.Rollback()
   193→
   194→	// Verify the version exists
   195→	var content string
   196→	err = tx.QueryRow(
   197→		`SELECT content FROM skill_versions WHERE skill_name = ? AND version = ?`,
   198→		skillName, version,
   199→	).Scan(&content)
   200→	if err == sql.ErrNoRows {
   201→		return fmt.Errorf("version %d not found for skill %s", version, skillName)
   202→	}
   203→	if err != nil {
   204→		return fmt.Errorf("reading version: %w", err)
   205→	}
   206→
   207→	// Deactivate all versions
   208→	_, err = tx.Exec(
   209→		`UPDATE skill_versions SET active = 0 WHERE skill_name = ?`,
   210→		skillName,
   211→	)
   212→	if err != nil {
   213→		return fmt.Errorf("deactivating versions: %w", err)
   214→	}
   215→
   216→	// Activate the target version
   217→	_, err = tx.Exec(
   218→		`UPDATE skill_versions SET active = 1 WHERE skill_name = ? AND version = ?`,
   219→		skillName, version,
   220→	)
   221→	if err != nil {
   222→		return fmt.Errorf("activating version: %w", err)
   223→	}
   224→
   225→	if err := tx.Commit(); err != nil {
   226→		return fmt.Errorf("committing transaction: %w", err)
   227→	}
   228→
   229→	// Write to disk
   230→	skillDir := filepath.Join(".claude", "skills", skillName)
   231→	if err := os.MkdirAll(skillDir, 0755); err != nil {
   232→		return fmt.Errorf("creating skill directory: %w", err)
   233→	}
   234→	skillPath := filepath.Join(skillDir, "SKILL.md")
   235→	if err := os.WriteFile(skillPath, []byte(content), 0644); err != nil {
   236→		return fmt.Errorf("writing skill file: %w", err)
   237→	}
   238→
   239→	return nil
   240→}
   241→
   242→// PrintJSON marshals v to JSON and writes it to stdout.
   243→func PrintJSON(v any) error {
   244→	enc := json.NewEncoder(os.Stdout)
   245→	enc.SetIndent("", "  ")
   246→	return enc.Encode(v)
   247→}
   248→

1→# Setup Redesign: Two-Path Onboarding Based on Outbound Maturity
     2→
     3→**Date:** 2026-03-23
     4→**Status:** Approved
     5→
     6→## Problem
     7→
     8→The current `/nurge:setup` skill runs a fixed 11-step linear flow for all users: connect Gmail → sync → classify → discover ICP → generate skills. It doesn't account for whether the user is starting outbound from scratch or has an existing setup with tools and data. These two scenarios need fundamentally different onboarding.
     9→
    10→## Design
    11→
    12→### Architecture: Single Skill, Branching Flow
    13→
    14→One `/nurge:setup` skill (v3.0.0) with a shared opening phase and two paths that converge at skill generation.
    15→
    16→### Phase 1: Shared Opening (All Users)
    17→
    18→Every user goes through the same foundation-building steps:
    19→
    20→| Step | What | Config Keys |
    21→|------|------|-------------|
    22→| 0. Name | "What should I call you?" | `user.name` |
    23→| 1. Company Discovery | Website → WebFetch → extract & confirm | `company.website`, `company.description`, `company.industry` |
    24→| 2. Role & Team | Role, solo vs team, team size | `user.role`, `team.size` |
    25→| 3. Prospect Geography | Where prospects are (sets compliance flags silently) | `prospects.geography` |
    26→| 4. Channel Reality | Transparent: "Nurge does email only right now" | — |
    27→| 5. Sending Infrastructure | Domain type, mailbox count, warmup status → sets sending limits | `sending.domain_type`, `sending.mailbox_count`, `sending.warmup_status` |
    28→| 6. Connect Email | `nurge connect gmail` always | — |
    29→| 7. KPIs | Goal + AI-suggested KPIs based on context, user tweaks | `kpi.goal`, `kpi.daily_emails`, `kpi.weekly_meetings`, `kpi.reply_rate_target` |
    30→| 8. The Fork | "Do you have an existing outbound setup?" + email data cross-reference | — |
    31→
    32→### Phase 2A: Starting Fresh (Nurge = Full Stack)
    33→
    34→For users with no CRM, no sequences, no process:
    35→
    36→| Step | What |
    37→|------|------|
    38→| A1. Sync & Learn | Sync email, classify — may find some outbound even if user says "fresh" |
    39→| A2. ICP from Assumptions + AI | User hypotheses + website + AI suggestions. Start narrow (one segment) |
    40→| A3. Buyer Personas | AI suggests 2-3 personas from ICP + product type. Title, pain points, messaging angle |
    41→| A4. Product Positioning | One-liner from website scrape, user iterates |
    42→| A5. Qualification Framework | Lightweight BANT, user tweaks |
    43→| A6. Sending Health Check | DNS (SPF/DKIM/DMARC) + warmup limits |
    44→| A7. Generate 13 Skills | Conversation-derived, improve as data accumulates |
    45→| A8. Summary & First Task | Based on goal |
    46→
    47→### Phase 2B: Existing Setup (Nurge Integrates)
    48→
    49→For users with tools, process, and email history:
    50→
    51→| Step | What |
    52→|------|------|
    53→| B1. Tool Inventory & Connect | HubSpot? Connect. Other CRM? Acknowledge, note for roadmap |
    54→| B2. Sync & Classify | Pull 90 days, classify — this is the goldmine |
    55→| B3. ICP from Real Data | Infer from email patterns, compare with assumptions |
    56→| B4. Buyer Personas from Data | Extract from email patterns (titles/roles emailed most) |
    57→| B5. Product Positioning | Website + email content analysis comparison |
    58→| B6. Qualification Framework | From pipeline data if HubSpot connected, complexity-appropriate |
    59→| B7. Analyze What's Working | `nurge context generate` — reply rates, best subjects, send times |
    60→| B8. Sending Health Check | DNS + reputation |
    61→| B9. Gap Analysis | What their stack lacks that Nurge fills natively |
    62→| B10. Generate 13 Skills | Data-derived, immediately high quality |
    63→| B11. Summary & First Task | Based on insights |
    64→
    65→### Convergence
    66→
    67→Both paths generate the same 13 skills:
    68→- Context: `nurge-product`, `nurge-icp`
    69→- Research: `nurge-research`, `nurge-qualify`
    70→- Workflow: `nurge-pipeline`
    71→- Action: `nurge-outbound`, `nurge-sequence`, `nurge-followup`, `nurge-replies`, `nurge-book`
    72→- Health: `nurge-hygiene`, `nurge-sending-health`
    73→- Measure: `nurge-analytics`
    74→
    75→The difference: Path A skills are conversation-derived (improve over time), Path B skills are data-derived (immediately strong).
    76→
    77→### All 17 Skills
    78→
    79→| Category | Skills |
    80→|----------|--------|
    81→| Context | `nurge-product`, `nurge-icp`, `nurge-personas` |
    82→| Research | `nurge-research`, `nurge-qualify` |
    83→| Workflow | `nurge-pipeline` |
    84→| Action | `nurge-outbound`, `nurge-sequence`, `nurge-followup`, `nurge-replies`, `nurge-book` |
    85→| Prospects | `nurge-prospects` |
    86→| Health | `nurge-hygiene`, `nurge-sending-health` |
    87→| Measure | `nurge-analytics`, `nurge-review` |
    88→
    89→See `docs/skills.md` for full documentation of each skill.
    90→
    91→### Current Capabilities (Honest Constraints)
    92→
    93→- **Channels:** Email only. LinkedIn and phone on roadmap.
    94→- **Connectors:** Gmail (Tier 1) and HubSpot (Tier 2) only.
    95→- **Be upfront** about these limits during setup. Don't ask about what we can't deliver.
    96→
    97→### KPI Benchmarks (From Research)
    98→
    99→| Scenario | Daily Emails | Reply Rate | Meetings/Week |
   100→|----------|-------------|------------|----------------|
   101→| Solo founder, fresh domain | 5 | — | 1 |
   102→| Solo founder, warmed | 20-30 | 5-10% | 2-3 |
   103→| Small team (3), warmed | 30-50/SDR | 15-25% | 8-12 total |
   104→| Enterprise-focused | 20-30/SDR | 10-15% | 4-6 total |
   105→
   106→### Data Flow
   107→
   108→- Every decision saved to config via `nurge config set` (skills read config)
   109→- Session hooks automatically capture the full conversation (learning system)
   110→- Config = structured decisions (queryable by skills)
   111→- Session logs = raw conversation (for learning/replay)
   112→- During classification: show summary counts only, not raw tables
   113→
   114→### Key Design Decisions
   115→
   116→1. **Name first** — personalization from the start
   117→2. **Website scrape does the heavy lifting** — minimal questions needed about company
   118→3. **ICP: data > assumptions** — Path B infers from real emails, Path A builds from hypotheses + AI
   119→4. **Buyer personas separate from ICP** — ICP = which companies, personas = which people
   120→5. **Qualification framework included** — even lightweight BANT helps skills decide what's worth pursuing
   121→6. **Sending limits tied to warmup** — prevents domain reputation damage
   122→7. **Compliance silent** — geography question sets flags, no GDPR lectures
   123→8. **One question at a time** — conversational, not a form
   124→
   125→## Files Changed
   126→
   127→- `~/.claude/skills/nurge-setup/SKILL.md` — complete rewrite (v2.0.0 → v3.0.0)
   128→- `internal/project/step_skill.go` — updated inlined skill content
   129→
   130→## Future Work
   131→
   132→- More connectors (Salesforce, Pipedrive, Apollo, etc.)
   133→- LinkedIn and phone channel support
   134→- Multi-domain sending infrastructure management
   135→- Team territory and handoff workflows
   136→

1→# Maturity-Based Setup: Split Flows by Outbound Maturity Level
     2→
     3→**Date:** 2026-03-23
     4→**Status:** Draft
     5→**Replaces:** Setup Redesign v3.0.0 (two-path fork)
     6→
     7→## Problem
     8→
     9→The current setup skill (v3.0.0) uses a binary fork: "starting fresh" vs "existing setup." Real outbound maturity is a spectrum across two independent axes — **data density** (email volume) and **tool maturity** (CRM, sequences, etc.). This creates mismatches:
    10→
    11→- A founder with 400 manual cold emails but no CRM gets "starting fresh"
    12→- A company with Salesforce full of inbound deals but zero outbound gets "existing setup"
    13→- A team of 10 SDRs with mature playbooks gets the same flow as a solo founder with HubSpot
    14→
    15→Additionally, the skill is a 377-line monolith that Claude must hold entirely in context regardless of which path is active.
    16→
    17→## Design
    18→
    19→### Maturity Model: 2x2 Detection
    20→
    21→Maturity is determined by two independent axes:
    22→
    23→```
    24→                    Low data (<200 outbound)    Mid data (200-499)     High data (500+)
    25→                   ┌───────────────────────┬──────────────────────┬──────────────────────┐
    26→No tools/empty CRM │  ZERO                 │  EXPERIMENTING       │  EXPERIMENTING       │
    27→                   │  (build everything)   │  (has signal, no sys)│  (has signal, no sys)│
    28→                   ├───────────────────────┼──────────────────────┼──────────────────────┤
    29→Has tools + CRM    │  EXPERIMENTING        │  EXPERIMENTING       │  OPERATING/SCALING   │
    30→                   │  (tools exist, no     │  (growing, building  │  (optimize + layer)  │
    31→                   │   outbound data)      │   the system)        │                      │
    32→                   └───────────────────────┴──────────────────────┴──────────────────────┘
    33→```
    34→
    35→The 200-499 range is the "growing" zone — enough data to learn from but not enough for confident pattern analysis. These users route to Experimenting regardless of tools, because the data isn't rich enough for Operating-level insights.
    36→
    37→Operating vs Scaling is split by team size: solo/small team = Operating, team >1 with multiple mailboxes = Scaling.
    38→
    39→### Edge Cases
    40→
    41→| Scenario | Emails | Tools | Route to | Rationale |
    42→|----------|--------|-------|----------|-----------|
    43→| True zero | 0 | None | Zero | Everything from scratch |
    44→| Has CRM, not connected | 0-50 | CRM exists but can't connect (e.g. Salesforce) | Zero | Can't verify data, treat as zero. Note CRM for future. |
    45→| Has CRM, connected but empty | 0-50 | CRM connected, no useful data | Experimenting | Connected CRM routes to experimenting. Phase 2 discovers it's empty and falls back to assumptions. |
    46→| Sent from personal email, no system | 50-200 | None | Experimenting | Has signal, no process |
    47→| Has CRM + sparse data | 50-200 | CRM with some deals | Experimenting | Partial data, fill gaps |
    48→| CRM with inbound deals, new to outbound (connected) | 0-50 outbound | CRM connected, has inbound data | Experimenting | CRM connected → experimenting. Phase 2 uses inbound data for ICP inference. |
    49→| Has tools, sends regularly, solo | 500+ | CRM + sequences | Operating | Working process, wants optimization |
    50→| Team, multiple mailboxes, mature | 1000+ | Full stack | Scaling | Wants intelligence layer across team |
    51→
    52→### File Structure
    53→
    54→```
    55→~/.claude/skills/nurge-setup/
    56→  SKILL.md                  # Router — detects maturity, delegates to right phase
    57→  phase-1-foundation.md     # Shared: name, company, role, infrastructure (all users)
    58→  phase-2-zero.md           # Zero outbound: warmup-first, build from scratch
    59→  phase-2-experimenting.md  # Some emails or tools, no system: hybrid approach
    60→  phase-2-operating.md      # Has data + tools: data-first optimization
    61→  phase-2-scaling.md        # Team with mature process: team-wide intelligence
    62→  skill-generation.md       # Shared: detailed guidance for generating all 17 skills
    63→```
    64→
    65→### SKILL.md (Router)
    66→
    67→The router is the only file Claude Code auto-loads. It:
    68→
    69→1. Runs prerequisite checks (`.nurge/` directory exists, `nurge connectors`)
    70→2. Checks resume state via `nurge config show --format json`
    71→3. Runs Phase 1 foundation (always)
    72→4. Detects maturity from signals
    73→5. Confirms with user: "[Name], based on what I see — [X emails, Y tools] — here's where you are: [description]. Sound right?"
    74→6. User confirms or overrides
    75→7. Tells Claude: "Read `phase-2-{level}.md` and follow it"
    76→
    77→Resume logic uses config keys to skip completed steps and detects which phase-2 file was in progress.
    78→
    79→### Phase 1: Foundation (All Users)
    80→
    81→Collected before maturity detection. Minimal questions to gather detection signals.
    82→
    83→| # | Step | What | Config keys | Detection signal? |
    84→|---|------|------|-------------|-------------------|
    85→| F1 | Name | "What should I call you?" | `user.name` | No |
    86→| F2 | Company discovery | Website → WebFetch → extract & confirm | `company.website`, `company.description`, `company.industry` | No |
    87→| F3 | Role & team | Role, solo vs team, team size | `user.role`, `team.size` | Yes — team size |
    88→| F4 | Tool inventory | Structured question with known options (see below) | `tools.crm`, `tools.sequences`, `tools.enrichment` | Yes — CRM, sequences, enrichment |
    89→| F5 | Connect email | `nurge connect gmail` | — | Yes — enables sync |
    90→| F6 | Sync & count | `nurge sync` → classify → count outbound | `detection.outbound_count` | Yes — email volume |
    91→| F7 | Maturity detection | Auto-detect from F3+F4+F6, confirm with user | `maturity.level` | This IS the detection |
    92→
    93→**F4 Tool Inventory — structured, not open-ended:**
    94→
    95→Ask three specific questions instead of one open-ended question:
    96→1. "Do you use a CRM?" → If yes: "Which one?" with options: HubSpot / Salesforce / Pipedrive / Other / None
    97→2. "Do you use a sequence or outreach tool?" → Options: Instantly / Lemlist / Smartlead / Apollo / Other / None
    98→3. "Do you use any enrichment tools?" → Options: Apollo / Clay / ContactOut / Other / None
    99→
   100→Save each answer: `nurge config set tools.crm "hubspot"`, `nurge config set tools.sequences "none"`, etc. This avoids free-text parsing.
   101→
   102→`has_crm` = `tools.crm` is not "none"
   103→`crm_connected` = CRM shows as connected in `nurge connectors` (currently only HubSpot supported)
   104→
   105→**F5 Connect Email — fallback if connection fails:**
   106→
   107→Always save `email.connected` after this step:
   108→- Success: `nurge config set email.connected true`
   109→- Failure (OAuth denied, network error, user uses Outlook):
   110→  - `nurge config set email.connected false`
   111→  - Detection proceeds with `outbound_count = 0`
   112→  - User is told: "We couldn't connect email yet. I'll set things up based on what you've told me, and we can connect later."
   113→  - Route to Zero or Experimenting based on tools + self-report alone
   114→  - Phase 2 checks `email.connected` and skips email-dependent steps if false
   115→
   116→**F6 Sync & Count:**
   117→
   118→Privacy consent before sync: "Nurge will send email subjects and snippets to Claude for classification. No full email bodies. Continue?"
   119→
   120→`nurge sync` pulls last 90 days. Then classify ALL emails in batches — this is a full classification, not a quick count. The classified data is reused by Phase 2 flows (E1, O2, S3 just present the results, they don't re-classify).
   121→
   122→Save: `nurge config set detection.outbound_count <number>`
   123→
   124→If sync fails or times out: proceed with whatever was synced. Partial data is better than no data.
   125→
   126→#### Detection Logic (at F7)
   127→
   128→```
   129→outbound_count = detection.outbound_count (from F6)
   130→has_crm = tools.crm != "none"
   131→crm_connected = check nurge connectors for active CRM
   132→team_size = config.team.size
   133→
   134→if outbound_count < 200 and not has_crm:
   135→    level = "zero"
   136→elif outbound_count < 200 and has_crm:
   137→    if crm_connected:
   138→        level = "experimenting"  # CRM connected, can check for data in Phase 2
   139→    else:
   140→        level = "zero"  # claimed CRM but not connected, treat as zero
   141→elif outbound_count >= 200 and outbound_count < 500:
   142→    level = "experimenting"  # growing zone, regardless of tools
   143→elif outbound_count >= 500 and has_crm and team_size > 1:
   144→    level = "scaling"
   145→elif outbound_count >= 500 and has_crm:
   146→    level = "operating"
   147→elif outbound_count >= 500 and not has_crm:
   148→    level = "experimenting"  # lots of emails but no system
   149→else:
   150→    level = "experimenting"  # defensive default
   151→```
   152→
   153→Key thresholds:
   154→- **<200 outbound**: Not enough data for pattern analysis. Zero or Experimenting based on tools.
   155→- **200-499 outbound**: Growing zone. Always Experimenting — data exists but isn't rich enough for Operating insights.
   156→- **500+ outbound**: Rich data. Operating or Scaling based on team size and tools.
   157→
   158→Note: `crm_has_deal_data` is NOT checked during detection. We only check if the CRM is connected. Whether it has useful data is discovered during Phase 2 (E4, O1) when we actually query it. This avoids needing a dedicated CLI command for CRM data checks.
   159→
   160→Override logic:
   161→- User says "starting fresh" but 400+ outbound → bump to experimenting: "Looks like you've sent some outbound already. Let me learn from that."
   162→- User overrides detected level during confirmation → respect their choice, save their override
   163→
   164→After detection: "[Name], based on what I see — [X outbound emails, Y tools] — [plain language description]. Sound right?" Let them confirm or override.
   165→
   166→### Phase 2 — Zero (No outbound, no tools)
   167→
   168→**Philosophy:** Infrastructure before messaging. Don't send until deliverability is validated.
   169→
   170→| # | Step | What |
   171→|---|------|------|
   172→| Z1 | Sending infrastructure | Domain type, mailbox count, warmup status. Fresh → hard limit 5/day. Primary → warn, recommend dedicated. |
   173→| Z2 | Deliverability check | `dig TXT` for SPF/DKIM/DMARC. Fix issues before anything else. Gates everything. |
   174→| Z3 | Channel reality | "Nurge does email only right now." Set expectations. |
   175→| Z4 | Prospect geography | Where prospects are → silent compliance flags. |
   176→| Z5 | ICP from assumptions | "Who do you want to sell to?" + website context. AI suggests structured ICP. ONE narrow segment. |
   177→| Z6 | Buyer personas | AI suggests 2-3 from ICP + product. Title, pain points, messaging angle. |
   178→| Z7 | Product positioning | One-liner from website scrape. Iterate until it clicks. |
   179→| Z8 | Social proof | Check website scrape. If none (likely): "No worries, we'll add proof as you land customers." Save: `nurge config set product.social_proof '<json or none>'` |
   180→| Z9 | Qualification framework | Lightweight BANT. |
   181→| Z10 | Outbound goals & KPIs | Conservative: 5 emails/day, 1-2 replies/week, 1 meeting/week. Warmup-constrained. |
   182→| Z11 | Generate skills | Read `skill-generation.md`. All 17. Conversation-derived. Flag they improve as data accumulates. |
   183→| Z12 | Live demo | Research a real company + person, draft first email (50-90 words). Activation moment. |
   184→
   185→**Key ordering:** Deliverability (Z2) before ICP (Z5). No point building ICP if emails don't land.
   186→
   187→### Phase 2 — Experimenting (Some signal, no system)
   188→
   189→**Philosophy:** Learn from what exists, fill gaps. They have signal — use it. Don't assume signal is clean or intentional.
   190→
   191→| # | Step | What |
   192→|---|------|------|
   193→| E1 | Present what exists | Classification already done in F6. Present the results: "You've sent X outbound to Y companies." No re-classification needed. |
   194→| E2 | Sending infrastructure | Same as Z1 but likely further along. Check warmup against actual volume. |
   195→| E3 | Deliverability check | DNS + check current sending health (bounce rates from email stats). |
   196→| E4 | CRM check | If CRM mentioned → connect if supported. If CRM has inbound data → use for ICP. If empty → note, move on. |
   197→| E5 | ICP: data + assumptions hybrid | Email patterns + CRM data (if any) + stated target. "You've been emailing [X types]. Intentional, or casting wide?" |
   198→| E6 | Buyer personas | Extract from email patterns (titles emailed) + supplement with AI. |
   199→| E7 | Product positioning | Website + how they describe product in sent emails. Compare. |
   200→| E8 | Social proof | Website + scan classified emails for proof points used. "You mentioned [customer] in 3 emails — keep using that?" Save: `nurge config set product.social_proof '<json or none>'` |
   201→| E9 | Qualification framework | BANT, informed by any CRM deal data. |
   202→| E10 | Prospect geography | From email data (where did they send?) + confirm. |
   203→| E11 | What's working (light) | Basic pattern analysis. Limited data = limited insights, be honest. |
   204→| E12 | Outbound goals & KPIs | Based on current volume + infrastructure. Stretch but realistic. |
   205→| E13 | Gap analysis (light) | "You don't have [sequences/analytics]. Nurge handles that." |
   206→| E14 | Generate skills | Read `skill-generation.md`. All 17. Hybrid-derived. |
   207→| E15 | Live demo | Use a pattern from their data if possible. |
   208→
   209→**Key difference from zero:** ICP comes from data + assumptions, not just assumptions. Positioning from how they already talk.
   210→
   211→### Phase 2 — Operating (Data + tools, wants optimization)
   212→
   213→**Philosophy:** Data-first. Show them things they don't know about their own outbound. Value = intelligence layer.
   214→
   215→| # | Step | What |
   216→|---|------|------|
   217→| O1 | Tool inventory & connect | CRM → connect. Note sequence tool, enrichment. Be honest about what integrates. |
   218→| O2 | Present email history | Classification already done in F6. Present full results: "[X] outbound to [Y] companies over [Z] months." This is the goldmine — show them the scale. |
   219→| O3 | Deliverability check | DNS + reputation signals + bounce rates. They've been sending — check health. |
   220→| O4 | ICP from real data | Infer from patterns. Compare with stated ICP. **Aha moment:** "You think Series B but best replies come from Series C." |
   221→| O5 | Buyer personas from data | Which titles they email, which respond, which convert. Data-driven prioritization. |
   222→| O6 | Product positioning alignment | Emails vs website. "In emails you say X, website says Y. Which converts?" |
   223→| O7 | Social proof from data | Extract from classified emails + website. "You drop [Customer] in 40% of emails — strongest proof?" Save: `nurge config set product.social_proof '<json>'` |
   224→| O8 | Analyze what's working | `nurge context generate` — full pattern analysis. Reply rates by persona, best subjects, send times. |
   225→| O9 | Qualification framework | From pipeline data if CRM connected. Deal velocity, complexity-appropriate. |
   226→| O10 | Outbound goals & KPIs | Data-informed. "You're at 8% reply rate, benchmark is 12-15%. Let's target 12%." |
   227→| O11 | Gap analysis | Stack gaps. Where Nurge layers intelligence vs replaces tools. |
   228→| O12 | Generate skills | Read `skill-generation.md`. All 17. Data-derived — immediately strong. |
   229→| O13 | Live demo | Lead with data insight, then action. "CTO emails convert 3x better. Here's one using your winning pattern." |
   230→
   231→**Key difference from experimenting:** Enough data for real insights. The "data vs assumptions" moment is activation.
   232→
   233→### Phase 2 — Scaling (Team, mature process)
   234→
   235→**Philosophy:** Team-wide intelligence. Cross-rep analysis, consistency, best-practice sharing.
   236→
   237→**Current limitation:** `nurge sync` syncs one Gmail account. Multi-mailbox sync is not yet built. Steps marked with `*` are aspirational — they collect team info via conversation and CRM data, but per-rep email analysis requires each rep to connect their own Gmail (future feature). The flow still delivers value through CRM pipeline analysis, shared playbook generation, and single-mailbox pattern insights that inform team strategy.
   238→
   239→| # | Step | What |
   240→|---|------|------|
   241→| S1 | Tool inventory & connect | Full stack audit. Connect what we can. |
   242→| S2 | Team structure | Who sends what, territory splits, shared vs individual mailboxes. Saved to config for skill generation — actual per-rep analysis is limited to connected mailbox + CRM data. |
   243→| S3 | Present email history | Classification from F6. Currently one mailbox only. If CRM connected, supplement with CRM activity data across reps. |
   244→| S4 | Deliverability audit | DNS check on all sending domains (user provides domain list). Per-mailbox health limited to connected account. |
   245→| S5 | ICP from data (segmented) | From connected mailbox + CRM data. Segment by company size, industry, time period. Per-rep segmentation available if CRM tracks owner. |
   246→| S6 | Buyer personas (team-wide) | Per-rep persona performance. Cross-team analysis. |
   247→| S7 | Product positioning | Consistency check. Is everyone positioning the same? Flag drift. |
   248→| S8 | Social proof inventory | Centralized proof points from website + connected mailbox emails + CRM win data. Save: `nurge config set product.social_proof '<json>'` |
   249→| S9 | Full performance analysis | Per-rep, per-segment, per-persona. Best practices from top performer → share. |
   250→| S10 | Qualification framework | From pipeline data. Stage and complexity appropriate. |
   251→| S11 | KPIs (team + individual) | Team targets + per-rep targets. Benchmarked against own data + industry. |
   252→| S12 | Gap analysis | Stack gaps + process gaps. |
   253→| S13 | Generate skills | Read `skill-generation.md`. All 17. Team-aware — reference team structure, territories, shared playbooks. |
   254→| S14 | Live demo | Team-level insight + individual action. "Team CTO conversion is 2x industry, but VP Eng lags. Here's a new angle based on Rep A's approach." |
   255→
   256→### skill-generation.md (Shared)
   257→
   258→Detailed guidance for generating all 16 project-local skills (the 17th is nurge-setup itself, already installed globally). Extracted so it's not duplicated across 4 flows.
   259→
   260→**Structure of skill-generation.md:**
   261→
   262→```markdown
   263→# Skill Generation Guide
   264→
   265→## File Structure
   266→Each skill MUST be a directory containing a SKILL.md file:
   267→.claude/skills/nurge-product/SKILL.md
   268→.claude/skills/nurge-icp/SKILL.md
   269→... (all 16 paths listed)
   270→
   271→## YAML Frontmatter
   272→Every SKILL.md must have:
   273→---
   274→name: nurge-<name>  # must match directory name
   275→description: <one-line>
   276→version: 1.0.0
   277→---
   278→
   279→## Quality by Maturity Level
   280→Read `nurge config show --format json` and check `maturity.level`:
   281→- zero: Skills are conversation-derived. Acknowledge data will improve them.
   282→- experimenting: Hybrid. Use available data, supplement with assumptions.
   283→- operating: Data-derived. Reference specific patterns from email analysis.
   284→- scaling: Data-derived + team-aware. Reference team structure, territories.
   285→
   286→## Per-Skill Instructions
   287→For each skill, reference `docs/skills.md` for full specification. Key highlights:
   288→
   289→### nurge-outbound (CRITICAL)
   290→- Emails MUST be 50-90 words max
   291→- Structure: 1 hook → 1-2 value sentences → 1 CTA
   292→- No filler, no preambles, no feature lists
   293→- Include word count validation
   294→- If social proof exists (check product.social_proof config):
   295→  weave ONE relevant proof point. Never fabricate.
   296→- Config: product.positioning, personas, prospects.geography, sending.*
   297→
   298→### nurge-product
   299→- Config: company.*, product.positioning, product.social_proof
   300→- Read company website data saved during setup
   301→
   302→### nurge-icp
   303→- Config: icp.*, prospects.geography
   304→- For operating/scaling: include data-derived insights
   305→
   306→... (each of the 16 skills gets a section)
   307→
   308→## Config Each Skill Reads
   309→[Table mapping each skill to its config dependencies]
   310→
   311→## What To Include In Every Skill
   312→- CLI commands the skill uses (nurge emails list, nurge send, etc.)
   313→- Config keys it reads
   314→- How to invoke related skills (e.g., nurge-outbound references nurge-research)
   315→```
   316→
   317→The full content of skill-generation.md is derived from `docs/skills.md` but formatted as actionable generation instructions rather than reference documentation.
   318→
   319→### Resume Logic
   320→
   321→Resume uses the same strategy everywhere: **check config keys to determine which steps are done** (declarative, not a step counter). This is robust against partial completions and crashes.
   322→
   323→The router (SKILL.md) checks:
   324→1. `setup_completed` → true? Don't run setup. Say: "[Name], setup is already complete. What would you like to do?"
   325→2. `maturity.level` → set? Phase 1 is done. Read the right phase-2 file.
   326→3. `maturity.level` not set? Check Phase 1 config keys, skip completed foundation steps.
   327→
   328→**Phase 1 resume keys:**
   329→
   330→| Config key exists | Skip step |
   331→|-------------------|-----------|
   332→| `user.name` | F1 |
   333→| `company.website` | F2 |
   334→| `user.role` AND `team.size` | F3 |
   335→| `tools.crm` | F4 |
   336→| `email.connected` or Gmail in `nurge connectors` | F5 |
   337→| `detection.outbound_count` | F6 |
   338→| `maturity.level` | F7 (entire detection) |
   339→
   340→**Phase 2 resume keys (per flow):**
   341→
   342→Each Phase 2 step saves a config key when complete. The phase file lists which config keys map to which steps. Example for Zero flow:
   343→
   344→| Config key exists | Skip step |
   345→|-------------------|-----------|
   346→| `sending.domain_type` AND `sending.warmup_status` | Z1 |
   347→| `sending.deliverability_checked` | Z2 |
   348→| (Z3 is informational — always show briefly) | — |
   349→| `prospects.geography` | Z4 |
   350→| `icp.company_size` | Z5 |
   351→| `personas` | Z6 |
   352→| `product.positioning` | Z7 |
   353→| `product.social_proof` | Z8 |
   354→| `qualification.framework` | Z9 |
   355→| `kpi.goal` AND `kpi.daily_emails` | Z10 |
   356→| Skills exist in `.claude/skills/nurge-*/` | Z11 |
   357→| `setup_completed` | Z12 |
   358→
   359→Each phase-2 file contains its own resume key table. Same pattern: check config keys, skip done steps, resume from first incomplete.
   360→
   361→If `maturity.level` is set but no Phase 2 config keys exist → Phase 1 is done, Phase 2 hasn't started. Greet with: "[Name], welcome back! We figured out where you are — let me pick up from there."
   362→
   363→### Go File Changes (step_skill.go)
   364→
   365→Instead of embedding 377 lines as a string literal:
   366→- Use Go `embed.FS` to embed separate `.md` files from a `skills/setup/` directory
   367→- Write each file to `~/.claude/skills/nurge-setup/`
   368→- Each file is focused, reviewable, and independently editable
   369→
   370→```
   371→internal/
   372→  project/
   373→    step_skill.go           # Embeds and writes all files
   374→    skills/
   375→      setup/
   376→        SKILL.md
   377→        phase-1-foundation.md
   378→        phase-2-zero.md
   379→        phase-2-experimenting.md
   380→        phase-2-operating.md
   381→        phase-2-scaling.md
   382→        skill-generation.md
   383→```
   384→
   385→### Data Flow
   386→
   387→- Every decision saved to config via `nurge config set` (skills read config)
   388→- Session hooks automatically capture full conversation (learning system)
   389→- `maturity.level` persisted so skills can reference it (e.g., nurge-review knows to set different expectations for zero vs operating)
   390→- Classification results stored in DB (via `nurge emails classify`)
   391→
   392→### KPI Benchmarks by Maturity
   393→
   394→| Level | Daily emails | Reply rate target | Meetings/week |
   395→|-------|-------------|-------------------|----------------|
   396→| Zero (fresh domain) | 5 | — | 1 |
   397→| Zero (warmed) | 20-30 | 5-10% | 2-3 |
   398→| Experimenting | 15-30 | 5-10% | 2-4 |
   399→| Operating (solo) | 30-50 | 10-15% | 4-6 |
   400→| Operating (small team) | 30-50/rep | 12-18% | 8-12 total |
   401→| Scaling | 30-50/rep | 15-25% | 15+ total |
   402→
   403→**Infrastructure constraints override maturity defaults.** An Experimenting user with a fresh domain gets 5 emails/day (same as Zero), not 15-30. The warmup status from the sending infrastructure step always caps the daily limit regardless of maturity level.
   404→
   405→### Current Capabilities (Honest Constraints)
   406→
   407→- **Channels:** Email only. LinkedIn and phone on roadmap.
   408→- **Connectors:** Gmail (Tier 1) and HubSpot (Tier 2) only.
   409→- **Be upfront** about these limits. Don't ask about what we can't deliver.
   410→
   411→### Error Handling for External Dependencies
   412→
   413→| Dependency | Failure mode | Fallback |
   414→|------------|-------------|----------|
   415→| WebFetch (F2, company website) | Site down, blocked, no useful content | Ask user to describe their company in 2-3 sentences. Save to config. Skill generation uses description instead of scrape. |
   416→| `nurge connect gmail` (F5) | OAuth denied, network error, user uses Outlook | Set `email.connected = false`. Detection uses `outbound_count = 0`. Phase 2 skips email-dependent steps. User can connect later. |
   417→| `nurge sync` (F6) | Timeout, partial sync, API rate limit | Use whatever was synced. Partial data > no data. Classification proceeds on available emails. |
   418→| `nurge connect hubspot` (E4, O1, S1) | OAuth fails, unsupported CRM | Note the failure, continue without CRM data. ICP/persona steps use email data + conversation only. |
   419→
   420→### Migration from v3.0.0
   421→
   422→Users who completed setup under v3.0.0 have config keys (`user.name`, `company.website`, `icp.*`, etc.) but no `maturity.level` or `tools.crm`. When the binary updates and overwrites skill files:
   423→
   424→- `setup_completed = true` → router says setup is complete, no re-run
   425→- If user wants to re-run: `nurge config set setup_completed false` then run `/nurge:setup` again
   426→- v3.0.0 config keys are compatible — Phase 1 resume detects them and skips completed steps
   427→- Missing keys (`tools.crm`, `maturity.level`, `detection.outbound_count`) trigger those steps to run, effectively re-detecting maturity
   428→
   429→No destructive migration needed. Existing configs work, new keys are additive.
   430→
   431→## Files Changed
   432→
   433→- `~/.claude/skills/nurge-setup/SKILL.md` — rewritten as router (v4.0.0)
   434→- `~/.claude/skills/nurge-setup/phase-1-foundation.md` — new
   435→- `~/.claude/skills/nurge-setup/phase-2-zero.md` — new
   436→- `~/.claude/skills/nurge-setup/phase-2-experimenting.md` — new
   437→- `~/.claude/skills/nurge-setup/phase-2-operating.md` — new
   438→- `~/.claude/skills/nurge-setup/phase-2-scaling.md` — new
   439→- `~/.claude/skills/nurge-setup/skill-generation.md` — new
   440→- `internal/project/step_skill.go` — rewritten to use embed.FS
   441→- `internal/project/skills/setup/*.md` — new embedded source files
   442→- `docs/skills.md` — update "Path A / Path B" references to 4-level maturity model
   443→
   444→## Future Work
   445→
   446→- More connectors (Salesforce, Pipedrive, Apollo, etc.)
   447→- LinkedIn and phone channel support
   448→- Multi-domain sending infrastructure management
   449→- Team territory and handoff workflows
   450→- Maturity re-assessment: as users accumulate data, suggest graduating to next level
   451→

1→package assembly
     2→
     3→import (
     4→	"database/sql"
     5→	"encoding/json"
     6→	"fmt"
     7→)
     8→
     9→// ResearchDossier is a per-prospect intelligence brief used during context assembly.
    10→type ResearchDossier struct {
    11→	ID            int             `json:"id"`
    12→	ContactEmail  string          `json:"contact_email"`
    13→	ContactName   string          `json:"contact_name,omitempty"`
    14→	CompanyDomain string          `json:"company_domain,omitempty"`
    15→	Dossier       json.RawMessage `json:"dossier"`
    16→	Tier          int             `json:"tier"`
    17→	ResearchDepth string          `json:"research_depth,omitempty"`
    18→	CreatedAt     string          `json:"created_at"`
    19→	UpdatedAt     string          `json:"updated_at"`
    20→}
    21→
    22→// SaveDossier inserts or updates a research dossier for a prospect.
    23→func SaveDossier(db *sql.DB, contactEmail, contactName, companyDomain string, dossier json.RawMessage, tier int, depth string) error {
    24→	if contactEmail == "" {
    25→		return fmt.Errorf("contact email is required")
    26→	}
    27→	if !json.Valid(dossier) {
    28→		return fmt.Errorf("dossier must be valid JSON")
    29→	}
    30→
    31→	_, err := db.Exec(`
    32→		INSERT INTO research_dossiers (contact_email, contact_name, company_domain, dossier, tier, research_depth)
    33→		VALUES (?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?)
    34→		ON CONFLICT(contact_email) DO UPDATE SET
    35→			contact_name = COALESCE(NULLIF(excluded.contact_name, ''), research_dossiers.contact_name),
    36→			company_domain = COALESCE(NULLIF(excluded.company_domain, ''), research_dossiers.company_domain),
    37→			dossier = excluded.dossier,
    38→			tier = excluded.tier,
    39→			research_depth = excluded.research_depth,
    40→			updated_at = datetime('now')
    41→	`, contactEmail, contactName, companyDomain, string(dossier), tier, depth)
    42→	if err != nil {
    43→		return fmt.Errorf("saving dossier: %w", err)
    44→	}
    45→	return nil
    46→}
    47→
    48→// GetDossier retrieves the research dossier for a prospect by email.
    49→func GetDossier(db *sql.DB, contactEmail string) (*ResearchDossier, error) {
    50→	var d ResearchDossier
    51→	var dossierStr string
    52→	err := db.QueryRow(`
    53→		SELECT id, contact_email, contact_name, company_domain, dossier, tier, research_depth, created_at, updated_at
    54→		FROM research_dossiers WHERE contact_email = ?
    55→	`, contactEmail).Scan(&d.ID, &d.ContactEmail, &d.ContactName, &d.CompanyDomain, &dossierStr, &d.Tier, &d.ResearchDepth, &d.CreatedAt, &d.UpdatedAt)
    56→	if err == sql.ErrNoRows {
    57→		return nil, nil
    58→	}
    59→	if err != nil {
    60→		return nil, fmt.Errorf("getting dossier: %w", err)
    61→	}
    62→	d.Dossier = json.RawMessage(dossierStr)
    63→	return &d, nil
    64→}
    65→
    66→// ListDossiers returns all dossiers, optionally filtered by company domain.
    67→func ListDossiers(db *sql.DB, domain string, limit int) ([]ResearchDossier, error) {
    68→	query := `SELECT id, contact_email, contact_name, company_domain, dossier, tier, research_depth, created_at, updated_at
    69→		FROM research_dossiers`
    70→	var args []any
    71→	if domain != "" {
    72→		query += ` WHERE company_domain = ?`
    73→		args = append(args, domain)
    74→	}
    75→	query += ` ORDER BY updated_at DESC`
    76→	if limit > 0 {
    77→		query += ` LIMIT ?`
    78→		args = append(args, limit)
    79→	}
    80→
    81→	rows, err := db.Query(query, args...)
    82→	if err != nil {
    83→		return nil, fmt.Errorf("listing dossiers: %w", err)
    84→	}
    85→	defer rows.Close()
    86→
    87→	var dossiers []ResearchDossier
    88→	for rows.Next() {
    89→		var d ResearchDossier
    90→		var dossierStr string
    91→		if err := rows.Scan(&d.ID, &d.ContactEmail, &d.ContactName, &d.CompanyDomain, &dossierStr, &d.Tier, &d.ResearchDepth, &d.CreatedAt, &d.UpdatedAt); err != nil {
    92→			return nil, fmt.Errorf("scanning dossier: %w", err)
    93→		}
    94→		d.Dossier = json.RawMessage(dossierStr)
    95→		dossiers = append(dossiers, d)
    96→	}
    97→	return dossiers, rows.Err()
    98→}
    99→
   100→// DeleteDossier removes a research dossier by contact email.
   101→func DeleteDossier(db *sql.DB, contactEmail string) error {
   102→	_, err := db.Exec(`DELETE FROM research_dossiers WHERE contact_email = ?`, contactEmail)
   103→	return err
   104→}
   105→

1→# Phase 2: Operating — Build the Prospect Intelligence Framework
     2→
     3→**Who:** 500+ outbound, CRM connected, solo or small team.
     4→**Data source:** Data-first. Rich email history + CRM pipeline. Show them what they don't know.
     5→**Philosophy:** Reveal hidden patterns, then add intelligence on top.
     6→
     7→## Resume Keys
     8→
     9→Same as phase-2-zero.md (S1-S10 steps use identical config keys).
    10→
    11→## Pre-Step: Deep Email Analysis
    12→
    13→With 500+ emails, present a full performance analysis before the intelligence build:
    14→
    15→"[Name], I analyzed [X] outbound emails. Here's what the data says:
    16→
    17→WHO REPLIES (not just who you email):
    18→| Persona         | Emails | Reply Rate |
    19→|-----------------|--------|------------|
    20→| [Title 1]       | [N]    | [X]%      |
    21→| [Title 2]       | [N]    | [X]%      |
    22→| [Title 3]       | [N]    | [X]%      |
    23→
    24→WHAT WORKS:
    25→- Subject lines that get opens: [pattern]
    26→- Openers that get replies: [pattern]
    27→- Best CTA style: [pattern]
    28→- Best day/time: [pattern]
    29→
    30→SURPRISES:
    31→- [Persona] replies at [X]% -- higher than [Persona] despite fewer emails
    32→- Emails mentioning [topic] get [X]x more replies
    33→- Your reply rate drops after [X] follow-ups
    34→
    35→Let me build your intelligence framework using these patterns."
    36→
    37→---
    38→
    39→## How Operating Differs From Zero
    40→
    41→The S1-S10 steps follow the **same structure** as phase-2-zero.md. Read that file for full step definitions. Below are ONLY the differences:
    42→
    43→### S1: Disqualifiers
    44→**DATA-DRIVEN:** "Companies in [industry/size] never reply to you (0% over [N] emails). Skip them?"
    45→
    46→### S2: ICP
    47→**FROM REPLY DATA, not just send data:**
    48→"You email VP Sales the most (340 emails) but RevOps replies at 15% vs VP Sales at 12%. Your real ICP might be different from who you think it is. Want to lean into what works?"
    49→
    50→### S3: Personas + Buying Committee
    51→**PART A personas:** Ranked by reply rate AND deal conversion (CRM data).
    52→"Your top 3 personas by actual conversion:
    53→1. RevOps -- 15% reply, 2.5% deal rate
    54→2. VP Sales -- 12% reply, 0.7% deal rate
    55→3. Founder -- 8% reply, 0.8% deal rate
    56→
    57→Surprise: RevOps converts 3.5x better than VP Sales."
    58→
    59→**PART B buying committee:** Rich CC/thread data from 500+ emails.
    60→"Your email threads show a clear buying committee pattern:
    61→- You email [Champion] first (100% of threads)
    62→- [Evaluator] gets CC'd after [X] exchanges ([Y]% of threads)
    63→- [Blocker] joins in [Z]% of threads (usually asks about [topic])
    64→- [Approver] appears in [W]% of threads (usually near close)
    65→
    66→Average thread length before deal: [N] emails across [M] people."
    67→
    68→### S4: Transformation Models
    69→**DATA-PROVEN patterns:**
    70→"For RevOps (your best-converting persona):
    71→- Your emails that convert mention 'data hygiene' and 'pipeline reporting'
    72→- You never pitch the product in first email -- you ask about their stack
    73→- Replies come when you name a specific tool they use
    74→
    75→Transformation model: 'RevOps spends 10+ hours/week cleaning CRM data.
    76→Your product automates the cleanup at the source.'
    77→
    78→This matches what works in your actual emails. Hit close?"
    79→
    80→### S5: Timing Signals
    81→**CALIBRATED by actual reply data:**
    82→"Here's what predicts replies in YOUR data:
    83→- Recently funded companies: 18% reply rate (vs 8% average)
    84→- Companies with >3 SDRs: 14% reply rate
    85→- Companies using [competitor]: 16% reply rate
    86→
    87→I'll prioritize these signals based on your proven data, not industry averages."
    88→
    89→### S6: Insight Priorities
    90→Same as zero, but validated: "Your emails with people insights (naming team members) get [X]x more replies than generic ones. Confirms people insight as #1 priority."
    91→
    92→### S7: Voice + Positioning
    93→**Fully extracted with performance correlation:**
    94→"Your voice profile from [X] emails:
    95→- Formality: [X]/10
    96→- Avg email: [X] words
    97→- Never uses: [patterns]
    98→- Always uses: [patterns]
    99→- Best opener: [specific pattern with reply rate]
   100→- Best CTA: '[exact phrase]' ([X]% reply rate vs [Y]% for alternatives)
   101→
   102→Your best-performing email: [show it with analysis of why it worked]"
   103→
   104→### S8-S10: Same as zero.
   105→

1→package email
     2→
     3→import (
     4→	"database/sql"
     5→	"fmt"
     6→)
     7→
     8→// CompactEmail has only the fields needed for classification — 4 fields instead of 11.
     9→type CompactEmail struct {
    10→	ID      string `json:"id"`
    11→	Subject string `json:"subject"`
    12→	From    string `json:"from"`
    13→	Snippet string `json:"snippet"`
    14→}
    15→
    16→// ListCompact returns emails with minimal fields for context-efficient AI consumption.
    17→func ListCompact(d *sql.DB, opts ListOptions) ([]CompactEmail, error) {
    18→	query := "SELECT id, subject, from_email, snippet FROM emails"
    19→	var args []any
    20→
    21→	if opts.Class != "" {
    22→		query += " WHERE class = ?"
    23→		args = append(args, opts.Class)
    24→	}
    25→
    26→	query += " ORDER BY date DESC"
    27→
    28→	if opts.Limit > 0 {
    29→		query += fmt.Sprintf(" LIMIT %d", opts.Limit)
    30→		if opts.Offset > 0 {
    31→			query += fmt.Sprintf(" OFFSET %d", opts.Offset)
    32→		}
    33→	}
    34→
    35→	rows, err := d.Query(query, args...)
    36→	if err != nil {
    37→		return nil, err
    38→	}
    39→	defer rows.Close()
    40→
    41→	var list []CompactEmail
    42→	for rows.Next() {
    43→		var e CompactEmail
    44→		var subj, snip sql.NullString
    45→		if err := rows.Scan(&e.ID, &subj, &e.From, &snip); err != nil {
    46→			return nil, err
    47→		}
    48→		e.Subject = subj.String
    49→		e.Snippet = snip.String
    50→		list = append(list, e)
    51→	}
    52→	return list, rows.Err()
    53→}

1→# Context Assembly Architecture: Design Spec
     2→
     3→**Date:** 2026-03-26
     4→**Status:** Draft
     5→**Scope:** Rewrite skill system from full SKILL.md files to thin wrappers + compiled context assembly + knowledge DB
     6→
     7→---
     8→
     9→## Problem
    10→
    11→Today, Nurge's 17 skills are full markdown files on disk (~2,000 tokens each). This creates three problems:
    12→
    13→1. **IP exposure:** Anyone can read `.claude/skills/nurge-outbound/SKILL.md` and copy the entire outbound methodology
    14→2. **Token waste:** Every skill loads its full content regardless of context. Writing an email to a Tier 3 marketing manager loads the same 4,900 tokens as a Tier 1 CTO
    15→3. **Learning rigidity:** The learning pipeline can only update at the skill-file level. Changing tone preferences means regenerating the entire outbound skill
    16→
    17→## Solution
    18→
    19→Three-layer architecture where the Go binary makes all deterministic decisions, the DB stores dynamic learnings, and skills are thin wrappers that invoke the binary.
    20→
    21→```
    22→Layer 0: Binary intelligence (compiled Go)
    23→  - Context assembly: which atoms to load for which task
    24→  - Tiering: ICP fit scoring algorithm
    25→  - Resolve: auto-detect input type, create contacts
    26→  - Enrichment: waterfall provider orchestration
    27→
    28→Layer 1: Knowledge DB (SQLite, per-user, dynamic)
    29→  - Tone preferences, hook patterns, positioning variants
    30→  - Research dossiers, A/B test results
    31→  - Strategic memory, session learnings
    32→
    33→Layer 2: Thin skills (SKILL.md, ~10-15 lines each)
    34→  - Bang command: !`nurge context assemble --task X --prospect $1`
    35→  - CLI actions: nurge send, nurge sequence, etc.
    36→  - Process instructions: present draft, wait for approval
    37→```
    38→
    39→---
    40→
    41→## 1. Knowledge DB Schema
    42→
    43→### New Tables
    44→
    45→```sql
    46→-- Atomic knowledge units: the building blocks of assembled context
    47→CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS knowledge_atoms (
    48→    id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,
    49→    atom_type TEXT NOT NULL,        -- tone, hooks, positioning, personas, objections, timing, compliance, structure
    50→    scope TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT '',  -- persona name, tier, geography, or empty for global
    51→    content TEXT NOT NULL,           -- JSON: the actual knowledge
    52→    source TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'setup',  -- setup, learning, strategic_memory, user
    53→    confidence REAL DEFAULT 1.0,    -- 0.0-1.0, decays over time without reinforcement
    54→    last_used_at TEXT DEFAULT '',
    55→    use_count INTEGER DEFAULT 0,
    56→    created_at TEXT DEFAULT (datetime('now')),
    57→    updated_at TEXT DEFAULT (datetime('now'))
    58→);
    59→
    60→CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_knowledge_atoms_type ON knowledge_atoms(atom_type);
    61→CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_knowledge_atoms_scope ON knowledge_atoms(atom_type, scope);
    62→CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_knowledge_atoms_confidence ON knowledge_atoms(confidence DESC);
    63→
    64→-- Research dossiers: per-prospect intelligence briefs
    65→CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS research_dossiers (
    66→    id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,
    67→    contact_email TEXT NOT NULL,
    68→    contact_name TEXT DEFAULT '',
    69→    company_domain TEXT DEFAULT '',
    70→    dossier TEXT NOT NULL,          -- JSON: triggers, pain points, messaging angle, sources
    71→    tier INTEGER DEFAULT 0,
    72→    research_depth TEXT DEFAULT '',  -- deep, light, none
    73→    created_at TEXT DEFAULT (datetime('now')),
    74→    updated_at TEXT DEFAULT (datetime('now')),
    75→    UNIQUE(contact_email)
    76→);
    77→
    78→CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_research_dossiers_email ON research_dossiers(contact_email);
    79→CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_research_dossiers_domain ON research_dossiers(company_domain);
    80→
    81→-- Enrichment cache: avoid re-querying providers for data we already have
    82→CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS enrichment_cache (
    83→    id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,
    84→    provider TEXT NOT NULL,         -- apify_linkedin, apollo, neverbounce, etc.
    85→    input_key TEXT NOT NULL,        -- hash of input params
    86→    input_data TEXT NOT NULL,       -- JSON: the query params
    87→    output_data TEXT NOT NULL,      -- JSON: the provider response
    88→    expires_at TEXT NOT NULL,       -- TTL for cache invalidation
    89→    created_at TEXT DEFAULT (datetime('now')),
    90→    UNIQUE(provider, input_key)
    91→);
    92→
    93→CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_enrichment_cache_lookup ON enrichment_cache(provider, input_key);
    94→CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_enrichment_cache_expiry ON enrichment_cache(expires_at);
    95→```
    96→
    97→### Contacts Table Extensions
    98→
    99→```sql
   100→-- New columns on existing contacts table (via migration)
   101→ALTER TABLE contacts ADD COLUMN tier INTEGER DEFAULT 0;
   102→ALTER TABLE contacts ADD COLUMN tier_score REAL DEFAULT 0;
   103→ALTER TABLE contacts ADD COLUMN tier_scored_at TEXT DEFAULT '';
   104→ALTER TABLE contacts ADD COLUMN enriched_at TEXT DEFAULT '';
   105→ALTER TABLE contacts ADD COLUMN enrichment_sources TEXT DEFAULT '[]';  -- JSON array
   106→ALTER TABLE contacts ADD COLUMN linkedin_url TEXT DEFAULT '';
   107→ALTER TABLE contacts ADD COLUMN website TEXT DEFAULT '';
   108→ALTER TABLE contacts ADD COLUMN funding_stage TEXT DEFAULT '';
   109→ALTER TABLE contacts ADD COLUMN company_size TEXT DEFAULT '';
   110→ALTER TABLE contacts ADD COLUMN industry TEXT DEFAULT '';
   111→ALTER TABLE contacts ADD COLUMN tech_stack TEXT DEFAULT '[]';  -- JSON array
   112→ALTER TABLE contacts ADD COLUMN signals TEXT DEFAULT '[]';     -- JSON array: job_change, funding, hiring, etc.
   113→ALTER TABLE contacts ADD COLUMN data_confidence REAL DEFAULT 0;  -- 0.0-1.0
   114→ALTER TABLE contacts ADD COLUMN resolve_source TEXT DEFAULT '';   -- email, csv, linkedin_person, linkedin_company, website, search, user_provided
   115→```
   116→
   117→### Knowledge Atom Types
   118→
   119→| Type | Scope examples | Content structure |
   120→|------|---------------|-------------------|
   121→| `tone` | `CTO`, `VP Sales`, `global` | `{"formality": "casual-professional", "avoided_phrases": [...], "voice_notes": "..."}` |
   122→| `hooks` | `tier_1`, `funding_trigger`, `job_change_trigger` | `{"patterns": [...], "examples": [...], "reply_rates": {...}}` |
   123→| `positioning` | `CTO`, `VP Eng`, `global` | `{"primary": "...", "variants": [...], "proof_points": [...]}` |
   124→| `personas` | `CTO`, `VP Sales` | `{"pain_points": [...], "messaging_angle": "...", "buying_triggers": [...]}` |
   125→| `objections` | `no_budget`, `have_solution`, `not_now` | `{"response_pattern": "...", "examples": [...]}` |
   126→| `timing` | `CTO`, `US`, `EU` | `{"best_days": [...], "best_hours": "...", "timezone_notes": "..."}` |
   127→| `compliance` | `US`, `EU`, `DE`, `FR` | `{"requirements": [...], "unsubscribe_text": "...", "legal_page": "..."}` |
   128→| `structure` | `first_touch`, `follow_up`, `reply` | `{"word_limit": [50, 90], "sections": [...], "cta_style": "..."}` |
   129→
   130→---
   131→
   132→## 2. Context Assembly Engine
   133→
   134→### New package: `internal/assembly/`
   135→
   136→```go
   137→// AssembleRequest is the input to the context assembler.
   138→type AssembleRequest struct {
   139→    Task     string // outbound, research, qualify, followup, reply, pipeline, sequence, analytics
   140→    Prospect string // email address or empty for non-prospect tasks
   141→    Format   string // markdown, json, text
   142→}
   143→
   144→// AssembleResult is the output: a focused prompt ready for Claude.
   145→type AssembleResult struct {
   146→    Context     string            // the assembled prompt text
   147→    TokenCount  int               // estimated token count
   148→    AtomsLoaded []string          // which atoms were included
   149→    Directives  []string          // e.g., "research_required", "confirm_company"
   150→    Cached      bool              // was this a cache hit?
   151→}
   152→```
   153→
   154→### Assembly Flow
   155→
   156→```
   157→AssembleRequest
   158→    |
   159→    v
   160→1. CLASSIFY TASK
   161→   - What type of email? (first-touch, follow-up, reply)
   162→   - Or non-email task? (research, pipeline, analytics)
   163→   |
   164→   v
   165→2. LOAD PROSPECT (if prospect provided)
   166→   - Query contacts table
   167→   - Check: enriched? tiered? has dossier?
   168→   - If not enriched: add "enrich_required" directive
   169→   - If not tiered: run tier scoring inline
   170→   - If no dossier and Tier 1/2: add "research_required" directive
   171→   |
   172→   v
   173→3. SELECT ATOMS
   174→   Based on task + prospect tier + persona + geography:
   175→   - Always: structure atom for this task type
   176→   - Always: tone atom for this persona (or global)
   177→   - First-touch: hooks atom for this tier + trigger type
   178→   - First-touch: positioning atom for this persona
   179→   - Follow-up: skip hooks, load follow-up structure
   180→   - Reply: load objection atom matching reply classification
   181→   - International: load compliance atom for geography
   182→   - Load relevant learnings from DB
   183→   |
   184→   v
   185→4. ASSEMBLE PROMPT
   186→   Combine atoms into structured markdown:
   187→
   188→   ## Context
   189→   [prospect data: name, title, company, tier]
   190→   [prior interactions if any]
   191→
   192→   ## Directives
   193→   [research_required / enrich_required / none]
   194→
   195→   ## Tone
   196→   [tone atom content]
   197→
   198→   ## Structure
   199→   [structure atom: word limits, sections, CTA style]
   200→
